content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

July 26, 1990


Nicholas J. Ross
Lindquest & Company
Certified Public Accountants
5000 Executive Parkway, Suite 400
San Ramon, CA  94583

Re:  Your Request for Advice
     Our File No. A-90-439

Dear Mr. Ross:

     We have received your letter dated June 26, 1990, seeking
advice regarding the responsibilities and duties of a broad based
recipient committee under the campaign financing and reporting
provisions of the Political Reform Act ("the Act").1

QUESTIONS

     1.  May a broad based general recipient committee make a
contribution of up to $1,000 to a candidate for state office in
another state?

     2.  If permissible under the Act, what are the committee's filing
responsibilities as a result of such a contribution?

CONCLUSIONS

     1.  A contribution by a broad based general recipient committee
of up to $1,000 to a candidate for state office in another state
is permissible under the Act.

     2.  The contribution should be reported as an expenditure on
Schedule E of Form 420.


_________________________
1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015.  All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.  Commission
Regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000, et seq.  All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.


428 J Street, Suite 800 ● P.O. Box 807 ● Sacramento CA 95804-0807 ● (916) 322-5660

Our File No.  A-90-439
Page 2

FACTS

One of your clients, a broad based general recipient
committee located in California, has been solicited for monetary
contributions by a candidate for the office of governor of the
state of Alaska.  Your client wishes to contribute up to $1,000 to
this campaign, but has requested your assistance in determining
the legality of such a contribution and the filing
responsibilities, if any, that such a contribution would create.

ANALYSIS

Although contributions by candidates' committees to
candidates in other states may be restricted under the Act, there
are no provisions of the Act which prohibit a broad based general
recipient committee from making such a contribution.

Sections 85800 et seq. govern the use of campaign funds held
by a broad based general recipient committee.  Section 85800(a)
provides that Chapter 5, Article 8 of the Act applies to any
committee which qualifies as a committee pursuant to subdivision
(a) of section 82013.  The standards of Section 82013(a) encompass
broad based general recipient committees.

The general rule applied to the expenditure of campaign funds
is that the expenditures must be related to a political,
legislative, or governmental purpose.  (Section 85801).  It
appears that the contribution envisioned by your client would be
related to the purposes specified by Section 85801.

The Commission is currently reviewing the applicability of
Section 85800 to each of the use-of-campaign-fund sections (Sections 85800 et seq.).  Accordingly, the advice given in this letter is subject to change and should not be relied upon for future
questions, but only for the specific situation described in your
letter.

As to the reporting requirement, assuming that the
committee files Form 420, the appropriate procedure would be to
report the contribution as an expenditure on Schedule E of that
form.

Should you have any further question regarding this matter,
please feel free to contact me at (916) 322-5901.

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel

By: Joseph Garcia
Counsel, Legal Division
